subject: ap-chemistry
title: Strontium Hydroxide Solubility & Common Ion Effect
stimulus:
Strontium hydroxide dissolves in water according to the following equation. The $K_{sp}$ expression for strontium hydroxide is provided.

$\text{Sr(OH)}_2(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Sr}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq)$, $K_{sp} = [\text{Sr}^{2+}][\text{OH}^-]^2$

parts.prompt: A student draws the particulate diagram shown to represent the ions present in an aqueous solution of $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$. (Water molecules are intentionally omitted.) Identify the error in the student's drawing.
parts.scoring_notes: From the dissolution equation, $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$ dissociates as: $\text{Sr(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Sr}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^-$. For every 1 $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ ion, there should be 2 $\text{OH}^-$ ions (2:1 ratio of OH⁻ to Sr²⁺). The error in the student's drawing is likely showing equal numbers of $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ and $\text{OH}^-$ ions (1:1 ratio instead of the correct 1:2 ratio), or showing only one OH⁻ per Sr²⁺.

parts.prompt:
The student prepares a saturated solution by adding excess $\text{Sr(OH)}_2(s)$ to distilled water and stirring until no more solid dissolves. The student then determines that $[\text{Sr}^{2+}] = 0.043$ M in the solution.

(i) Calculate the value of $[\text{OH}^-]$ in the solution.

(ii) Calculate the value of $K_{sp}$ for $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated value of $[\text{OH}^-]$: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
From the dissolution equation: $\text{Sr(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Sr}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^-$.
For every 1 mol $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ dissolved, 2 mol $\text{OH}^-$ are released.
$[\text{OH}^-] = 2 \times 0.043\text{ M} = 0.086\text{ M}$

wrong_examples: Using $[\text{OH}^-] = 0.043/2 = 0.0215$ M — inverting the ratio; OH⁻ is produced in greater quantity.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $\frac{0.043\text{ mol Sr}^{2+}}{1\text{ L}} \times \frac{2\text{ mol OH}^-}{1\text{ mol Sr}^{2+}} = 0.086\text{ M OH}^-$.

sample_responses.commentary: Applies the 2:1 stoichiometry from the dissolution equation directly; matches the rubric.
Ignores that each Sr(OH)₂ formula unit releases two OH⁻ ions; misses the factor of 2.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated $K_{sp}$ value, consistent with (b)(i): 1 point
wrong_examples: Computing $(0.043)(0.086) = 3.7 \times 10^{-3}$ without squaring $[\text{OH}^-]$ — the exponent 2 in the $K_{sp}$ expression must be applied.
Using $[\text{OH}^-] = 0.043$ M (ignoring the factor of 2) — must use the value from (b)(i).
common_traps: $K_{sp} = [\text{Sr}^{2+}][\text{OH}^-]^2$. Square the OH⁻ concentration. Follow through from (b)(i) value of $[\text{OH}^-]$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value, consistent with (b)(i): 1 point. $K_{sp} = [\text{Sr}^{2+}][\text{OH}^-]^2 = (0.043)(0.086)^2 = 3.2 \times 10^{-4}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct $K_{sp}$ expression with the OH⁻ concentration squared; matches the rubric value.
Forgets to square [OH⁻]; the stoichiometric coefficient 2 must become an exponent.

parts.prompt: The student prepares a second saturated solution of $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$ in aqueous 0.10 M $\text{Sr(NO}_3)_2$ instead of water. Will the value of $[\text{OH}^-]$ in the second solution be greater than, less than, or equal to the value in the first solution? Justify your answer. (Assume constant temperature.)
parts.scoring_notes: Less than. The $\text{Sr(NO}_3)_2$ solution already contains $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ ions at 0.10 M (common ion). According to Le Chatelier's principle, the added $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ suppresses the dissolution of $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$ (common ion effect), shifting the equilibrium to the left. Less $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$ dissolves → less $\text{OH}^-$ is produced → $[\text{OH}^-]$ is lower than in the distilled water solution.

scoring_points.description: For 'less than' with correct common ion effect justification: 1 point

required_elements: The $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ from $\text{Sr(NO}_3)_2$ is a common ion that suppresses dissolution of $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$, resulting in less $\text{OH}^-$ being produced.
alternatives.correct_example:
Less than. The $\text{Sr(NO}_3)_2$ solution provides $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ ions (0.10 M), which is already present in the $K_{sp}$ expression. By Le Chatelier's principle, the additional $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ (common ion) shifts the dissolution equilibrium to the left:
$\text{Sr(OH)}_2(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Sr}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq)$

Less $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$ dissolves, producing fewer $\text{OH}^-$ ions. Therefore, $[\text{OH}^-]$ in the second solution is less than 0.086 M.

wrong_examples: Saying greater than — more $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ present suppresses dissolution; does not increase $[\text{OH}^-]$.
Saying equal to — constant temperature means constant $K_{sp}$, but the ion product $Q$ with added $\text{Sr}^{2+}$ shifts equilibrium left, changing $[\text{OH}^-]$.
common_traps: Common ion effect: adding a soluble salt with an ion already in the equilibrium reduces solubility. $K_{sp}$ is unchanged (temperature is constant), but equilibrium shifts left because $Q > K_{sp}$ when $[\text{Sr}^{2+}]$ is already 0.10 M. Less $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$ dissolves → lower $[\text{OH}^-]$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point. Less than. Because the Sr(NO₃)₂(aq) solution already contains a common ion, Sr²⁺(aq), the solubility of Sr(OH)₂ will be decreased, resulting in a lower value of [OH⁻].

sample_responses.response: Less than. Sr(NO₃)₂(aq) already contains the common ion Sr²⁺, which decreases the solubility of Sr(OH)₂, so [OH⁻] is lower than in the pure-water solution.
sample_responses.commentary: Matches the rubric phrasing — identifies Sr²⁺ as the common ion and links common-ion suppression to lower [OH⁻].

sample_responses.response: Greater than, because more Sr²⁺ in solution means more Sr(OH)₂ dissolves.
sample_responses.commentary: Reverses the common-ion effect — added Sr²⁺ shifts equilibrium toward Sr(OH)₂(s), reducing (not increasing) [OH⁻].
